1: ki naar Yisrael vaehavuhu umimitzraim qarati livni: 'When Israel was a child, I loved him, and out of Egypt I called my son.' Matthew's citation of Hos 11:1 in Matt 2:15 is one of the NT's most discussed typological uses of the OT — because Hosea 11:1 in its original context is clearly not a predictive prophecy but a historical statement about the Exodus. Matthew's method is typological recapitulation: Jesus is the new Israel, the true Son of God who goes down to Egypt and is called out again; what happened to the nation in type is fulfilled in the person of the Messiah in antitype. This reading was not arbitrary: it was grounded in the OT's identification of Israel as YHWH's 'son' (Exod 4:22-23) and the expectation that the Messiah would recapitulate and fulfill Israel's story.
2: qereu lahem ken halchu mipaneihem labbe'alim yezabechu: 'The more they were called, the more they went away; they kept offering sacrifices to the Baals.' The calling (qare'u, they called/were called) that should have drawn Israel toward YHWH produced the opposite response — the more the covenant summons, the more the flight. This is the hardening dynamic of disobedience: repeated calls that are rejected do not soften the heart but harden it. The Baals received their sacrifices while YHWH's calls went unanswered — the inverted covenant response.
3: va'anochi riggalti leEfraim qacham al zero'otav: 'Yet it was I who taught Ephraim to walk, taking him by the arms.' The divine father who teaches the child to walk (taking the arms for support) is one of the OT's most tender images of the YHWH-Israel relationship. Riggalti (I taught to walk, I trained the feet) is a rare verb describing the most intimate parental instruction. 'But they did not know that I healed them' (lo yade'u ki refa'tim) — the child who does not recognize the healer, taking the restored health as normal rather than as divine gift. Ingratitude as blindness: the healed do not know their healer.
4: bechavlei adam emshachem ba'avot ahavah: 'I led them with cords of human kindness, with ropes of love.' Chavel adam (cords of a human being / cords of human kindness) and avot ahavah (ropes of love) — the divine leading is not coercion by force but the gentle drawing of covenant love. 'I was like someone who lifts the yoke from their jaws, and I bent down to them to feed them' — the image of an owner caring for a laboring animal, relieving the burden at the jaw and bending to provide food. The condescension of divine care — YHWH stoops to feed the one he has led — is the OT form of John 13:4-5 (Christ bending to wash feet).
5: lo yashov el Eretz Mitzraim veAshur hu malko: 'He will not return to Egypt; the Assyrian will be his king, because they refused to repent.' The exile direction is Assyria, not Egypt — but the theological meaning is the same as a return to Egypt: subjugation under a foreign king. 'They refused to repent' (ki me'enu lashuv) — shuv (return/repent) is the covenant response Hosea has called for throughout; its refusal determines the exile. The exile is not arbitrary but is the direct consequence of refusing the return that would have prevented it.
6: vechalah cherev be'arav vekiletah badav ve'achelah mimo'atzoteihem: 'A sword will rage against his cities; it will devour his strongholds and consume them because of their schemes.' The sword (cherev) devouring the fortified cities is the military expression of covenant judgment. The schemes (mo'etzot, counsels, plans) that trusted in foreign alliances rather than in YHWH are what the sword consumes — judgment falls precisely on the human strategies designed to avoid it.
7: ve'ammi teluvim litshuvati: 'My people are determined to turn away from me.' Teluvim litshuvati is grammatically difficult: possibly 'bent on turning away from me' or 'hanging/suspended in returning to me.' The tension in 11:7-9 is the theological center of the chapter: the people's determination to turn away vs. YHWH's determination not to give them up (v8). 'Though they call out to the Most High (el*Al*), none of them will lift him up' — even when they call to YHWH, their calls are ineffective because they do not actually turn.
8: ech eten'cha Efraim amasger'cha Yisrael: 'How can I give you up, Ephraim? How can I hand you over, Israel?' This verse is the emotional and theological peak of Hosea — the divine pathos at its most intense. The rhetorical questions are not requests for an answer but expressions of divine anguish over the covenant relationship. Admah and Tzevoim were cities destroyed with Sodom (Gen 14:2; Deut 29:23) — to make Ephraim like them would be their complete destruction. 'My heart turns (nehpak libbi) within me; all my compassions are stirred' (nichamu nichumi) — the divine heart literally 'overturns' at the prospect of Israel's total destruction. This is the OT's most concentrated expression of divine mercy wrestling with justice.
9: lo e'eseh charon api lo ashuv leshachet Efraim: 'I will not carry out my fierce anger; I will not again destroy Ephraim.' The covenant-breaking people merit destruction, yet YHWH restrains the full expression of wrath. 'For I am God and not a human being' (ki El anochi velo ish) — the transcendence of divine love over human patterns of retribution. A human being given up on would stay given up on; YHWH's nature as God allows him to continue loving past what human love would endure. 'The Holy One (qadosh) in your midst' — holiness here is not primarily moral purity but the divine otherness that enables covenant faithfulness past all human expectation. This is the OT foundation for the NT's 'God is love' (1 John 4:8) — the divine nature, not human sentiment, is the ground of the inexhaustible mercy.
10: acharei YHWH yelchu kearyeh yish'ag: 'They will walk after the LORD; he will roar like a lion. When he roars, his children will come trembling from the west.' The lion-roar is a sound of authority and summons (cf. Amos 3:8: 'the lion has roared; who will not fear?'). Here YHWH's roar summons the exiles home — the same power that sounded as judgment now sounds as restoration-call. The trembling (yecheredu) that was previously fear of judgment is transformed into the trembling of children rushing to their father. The scattering described throughout ch. 9-10 begins to reverse.
11: yecherdu ketzipur miMitzraim ukeyonah me'Eretz Ashur: 'They will come trembling like birds from Egypt and like doves from Assyria.' The same dove that in 7:11 was senseless and directionless — flying to Egypt and Assyria in political panic — here comes from those same places back to YHWH. The return migration of the dove reverses the earlier flight. 'I will settle them in their homes' (vehoshavtim al bateihem) — the covenant promise of land-as-home is restored; the exile's dispossession is reversed by YHWH's direct resettlement.
12: svavuni vekachash Efraim uvemiremah beit Yisrael: 'Ephraim has surrounded me with lies, and the house of Israel with deceit.' The surrounding (svavuni) of YHWH with lies — Israel's covenant words that profess relationship while enacting betrayal — is the final charge of ch. 11 before the historical reflection of ch. 12. 'But Judah still walks with God and is faithful with the Holy Ones' — the contrast between Judah and Ephraim in 11:12 is the moment of relative credit for Judah before Hosea returns to Jacob-history as the common ancestor's story.
